chunk_summary: Hybrid effectiveness-implementation designs integrate clinical impact and implementation strategies, aiding nutrition practice by addressing effectiveness and real-world uptake challenges.
learning_objectives: Understand the three types of hybrid effectiveness-implementation designs and their applications in nutrition practice.
Learn how to select the appropriate hybrid design based on clinical and implementation priorities.
Identify key outcomes for clinical and implementation research in nutrition interventions.
Explore the use of frameworks like CFIR and RE-AIM for guiding implementation research.
key_facts: Type 1 hybrid focuses on clinical effectiveness with secondary implementation outcomes; useful for early feasibility and fidelity data.
Type 2 hybrid has dual aims of effectiveness and implementation; requires pre-specification of clinical (e.g., growth, HbA1c) and implementation outcomes (e.g., adoption, cost).
Type 3 hybrid prioritizes implementation with secondary clinical outcomes; suitable for interventions with strong evidence but poor uptake (e.g., ORS + zinc for diarrhea).
Frameworks like CFIR and RE-AIM help guide implementation research and ensure generalizability.
Implementation units can include providers, clinics, or schools, and adaptation documentation is critical for scalability.
reference_quote: "Effectiveness-implementation hybrid designs: combining elements of clinical effectiveness and implementation research."
